title: How To Train Your Researcher
text: Click here if you are not redirected automatically.

title: Omniscient Reader’s Viewpoint (ORV)
text:
Reading research papers is a skill — it takes deliberate practice and good habits. This guide compiles advice from graduate students and professors on how to approach AI research papers and academic articles efficiently and critically.






Note



This isn’t a page that tells you “how to read a research paper”. It’s more of a “you can try these tips because it has worked for us” kind of page. Learning when to skim and when to read deeply is part of the skill which you should apply to this page as well!






Know why you are reading. Are you looking for related work, a specific method, or a broad survey of a field / problem (e.g., AI hallucinations)? Your goal determines how broad or deep you go.
Check the venue. NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, EMNLP — knowing the conference gives you a sense of the paper’s quality and focus area.
Read the abstract twice. Once to understand the claim; once to identify what you should be looking for in the body.

title: Omniscient Reader’s Viewpoint (ORV)
text:
Know why you are reading. Are you looking for related work, a specific method, or a broad survey of a field / problem (e.g., AI hallucinations)? Your goal determines how broad or deep you go.
Check the venue. NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, EMNLP — knowing the conference gives you a sense of the paper’s quality and focus area.
Read the abstract twice. Once to understand the claim; once to identify what you should be looking for in the body.

title: Omniscient Reader’s Viewpoint (ORV)
text:
Critical Questions to Ask

What is the core problem being solved?
What is the key insight or novelty?
What assumptions does the method rely on?
How strong is the experimental validation?
What are the limitations the authors acknowledge — and those they don’t?

title: Omniscient Reader’s Viewpoint (ORV)
text:
Starting Points
Part of my research journey was figuring out which conferences are considered “reputable” and/or “impactful”. While this list is non-exhaustive, I listed some of them here to help you (hopefully an aspiring AI researcher) find a starting point.






Note



AI is often cross disciplinary! If you are working on such projects, please do your homework to find out which other venues — both conferences and journals — are considered strong/impactful for your field.

title: How To Train Your Researcher (HTTYR)
text:
This page is specific to the Early Research Opportunity Program (EROP) at SUTD. It walks mentors and students through the “I Do, We Do, You Do” teaching strategy — a scaffolded approach that progressively transfers responsibility from mentor to student.






Note



These guides are living documents. They are meant to reduce friction, not to be treated as the definitive rulebook for doing research.

title: How To Train Your Researcher (HTTYR)
text:
This page is specific to the Early Research Opportunity Program (EROP) at SUTD. It walks mentors and students through the “I Do, We Do, You Do” teaching strategy — a scaffolded approach that progressively transfers responsibility from mentor to student.






Note



These guides are living documents. They are meant to reduce friction, not to be treated as the definitive rulebook for doing research.

title: How To Train Your Researcher (HTTYR)
section: The “I Do, We Do, You Do” Framework
text:
The “I Do, We Do, You Do” Framework
The framework breaks mentorship into three phases of decreasing mentor involvement and increasing student autonomy.

I Do — Modelling
The mentor demonstrates the skill or process while thinking aloud. Students observe and ask questions.
Example: > The mentor reads an AI research paper aloud, narrating the decisions they make > as they skim the abstract, identify the key contribution, and assess > experimental validity.


We Do — Guided Practice
The mentor and student work through a task together. The student takes the lead; the mentor provides real-time prompts and feedback.
Example: > The student reads a new paper while the mentor asks guiding questions: > “What problem is this solving?”, “What baseline did they compare against?”, > “Would this method work in a different domain?”


You Do — Independent Practice
The student completes the task independently and presents their work. The mentor reviews and gives structured feedback afterward.
Example: > The student independently reads and summarises three papers from a reading list, > then presents a brief synthesis to the mentor in the next meeting.

SSS Class Content Creator (3SC) — tips on academic writing

title: SSS Class Content Creator (3SC)
text:
Academic writing is built from small, repeatable techniques. This page is a collection of these techniques (collectively known as writing advice) that an aspiring research might find useful. We hope that the contents here would be useful for you (the aspiring researcher) in becoming more aware of these sentence/paragraph-level patterns and to use them effectively for structuring arguments, signal relationships between ideas, and guiding readers through your article.






Note



These are techniques, not rules. Learn the pattern first, then develop your own voice around it.

title: SSS Class Content Creator (3SC)
text:
Topic Sentences
Every paragraph should open with a topic sentence that states the paragraph’s single claim. The rest of the paragraph supports it.
Weak opening (announces the topic, not a claim): > “This section discusses the limitations of our approach.”
Strong opening (makes a claim): > “Our approach has three limitations that constrain its applicability to > low-resource settings.”

title: SSS Class Content Creator (3SC)
